event: Early hominins inhabit North Africa, with Oldowan-style stone tools dating to roughly 1.8 million years ago found at Ain Boucherit in neighboring Algeria, reflecting the region's deep human prehistory.
Aterian Middle Stone Age cultures, known for distinctive tanged tools, develop across the Maghreb including the area of present-day Tunisia.
Capsian culture, a major Mesolithic tradition named after the Tunisian town of Gafsa (Capsa), flourishes across the region.
Phoenician traders from Tyre establish trading posts along the Tunisian coast, including Utica, founded by tradition around this time.
Phoenician colonists, led by tradition by Queen Dido (Elissa), found the city of Carthage near modern Tunis.
Carthage and the early Roman Republic conclude their first recorded treaty defining spheres of trade and influence.
Carthage is defeated by the Greeks of Sicily at the Battle of Himera, checking its expansion in the western Mediterranean.
The First Punic War erupts between Carthage and Rome over control of Sicily.
The Carthaginian general Hannibal launches the Second Punic War, crossing the Alps to invade Italy.
Rome's Scipio Africanus defeats Hannibal at the Battle of Zama in present-day Tunisia, ending the Second Punic War.
Rome destroys Carthage at the end of the Third Punic War and annexes its territory as the province of Africa.
Julius Caesar defeats the Pompeians at the Battle of Thapsus, consolidating Roman control over Africa.
Augustus refounds Carthage as a Roman colony, and it grows into one of the great cities of the empire.
The Scillitan Martyrs are executed near Carthage, providing one of the earliest dated records of Christianity in Roman Africa.
The African province revolts and proclaims Gordian I emperor, sparking a crisis in the wider Roman Empire.
The Donatist schism deepens in Roman Africa, dividing the North African Christian church for decades.
The Vandals under King Geiseric capture Carthage and make it the capital of their North African kingdom.
The Byzantine general Belisarius conquers the Vandal kingdom, returning the region to Roman (Byzantine) rule.
Arab Muslim armies under Uqba ibn Nafi found Kairouan, which becomes a key center of Islam in the Maghreb.
Arab forces capture Carthage from the Byzantines, completing the Muslim conquest of the region known as Ifriqiya.
Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab founds the Aghlabid dynasty, establishing a largely autonomous emirate based at Kairouan.
The Fatimids overthrow the Aghlabids and establish their Shia caliphate in Ifriqiya before moving to Egypt.
The Zirid rulers break with the Fatimids, prompting the destructive migration of the Banu Hilal Arab tribes into the region.
The Almohads incorporate Ifriqiya into their empire, briefly unifying much of North Africa and Iberia.
Abu Zakariya founds the Hafsid dynasty, which rules Tunisia as an independent and prosperous state for over three centuries.
Louis IX of France dies of disease during the Eighth Crusade laid against Hafsid Tunis.
Holy Roman Emperor Charles V captures Tunis from the Ottoman corsair Barbarossa, briefly restoring a Hafsid client ruler.
The Ottomans conquer Tunis definitively, ending the Hafsid dynasty and incorporating Tunisia into their empire.
Hussein ibn Ali founds the Husainid dynasty of beys, who rule Tunisia under nominal Ottoman suzerainty until 1957.
Under European pressure following an Anglo-Dutch bombardment of Algiers, Tunisia begins curbing Barbary corsair piracy and the trade in Christian captives.
Ahmad Bey abolishes slavery in Tunisia, one of the earliest such measures in the Muslim world.
Tunisia promulgates a constitution, among the first in the Arab world, establishing limited constitutional government.
Crippled by debt, Tunisia accepts an international financial commission of France, Britain, and Italy to oversee its finances.
France invades Tunisia and imposes the Treaty of Bardo, establishing a French protectorate during the Scramble for Africa.
Nationalists found the Destour (Constitution) Party to demand greater rights and self-government under the protectorate.
Habib Bourguiba leads a split from the Destour to form the more activist Neo-Destour party.
Axis forces occupy Tunisia, making it a major battleground of the North African campaign of World War II.
Allied forces win the Tunisia Campaign, expelling Axis troops from North Africa in May.
Tunisia gains independence from France on 20 March and joins the United Nations the same year.
Tunisia abolishes the monarchy, deposing the last bey, and Habib Bourguiba becomes the republic's first president.
Tunisia is a founding member of the Organization of African Unity, the forerunner of the African Union.
Bourguiba is declared president for life, cementing one-party rule under his Neo-Destour (later Socialist Destourian) party.
Prime Minister Zine El Abidine Ben Ali removes the ailing Bourguiba in a bloodless coup and assumes the presidency.
An al-Qaeda truck bombing at the Ghriba synagogue on Djerba kills more than 20 people, mostly foreign tourists.
The self-immolation of street vendor Mohamed Bouazizi in Sidi Bouzid in December ignites protests that spark the Arab Spring.
Mass protests force President Ben Ali to flee into exile in January, ending his rule in the Jasmine Revolution.
Tunisia adopts a new democratic constitution and holds free presidential and parliamentary elections.
The Tunisian National Dialogue Quartet wins the Nobel Peace Prize for guiding the country's democratic transition amid deadly terrorist attacks at the Bardo Museum and in Sousse.
President Kais Saied suspends parliament and seizes broad powers, in a move critics call a coup that erodes Tunisia's democracy.
Saied pushes through a new constitution by referendum that concentrates power in the presidency.
